title: Semantics for Rational Consequence — Ranked Preferential Models

questions.text: Why were ranked preferential models introduced in this module? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: To provide a semantic definition of rational consequence that is sound and complete with respect to the GM proof theory
The Gabbay–Makinson rules define rational consequence only syntactically, and a semantic counterpart was needed
To replace the GM rules entirely with a model-theoretic approach that abandons the six axioms
To mirror the relationship between classical propositional logic and its model-theoretic semantics

explanations.text: The motivation is soundness and completeness of the semantics with respect to the proof theory.
explanations.notes_quote: we want a semantic definition that is sound and complete with respect to the proof theory. Ranked preferential models provide exactly such a semantics.

explanations.text: The GM rules are a purely syntactic characterisation; ranked models supply the semantic side.
explanations.notes_quote: rational consequence relations have been defined purely syntactically — via the Gabbay–Makinson (GM) rules

explanations.text: Ranked models complement the GM rules; they do not replace or abandon them. The Representation Theorem shows equivalence.
The analogy with classical logic is explicit in the motivation.
explanations.notes_quote: as in classical propositional logic, we want a semantic definition that is sound and complete with respect to the proof theory.

questions.text: Which statements correctly describe a ranked preferential model $\vec{S}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\vec{S} = \langle S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k \rangle$ is an ordered sequence of length $k \geq 1$
Each $S_i$ is a set of propositional valuations
$S_1$ contains the most usual worlds; $S_k$ contains the most exceptional
Every possible propositional valuation must appear in at least one $S_i$
Valuations deemed impossible need not appear in any $S_i$

explanations.text: This is the formal statement of Definition 2.1.
explanations.notes_quote: A ranked preferential model is an ordered sequence $\vec{S} = \langle S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k \rangle$ of length $k$, for some $k \geq 1$

explanations.text: Each layer consists of propositional valuations.
explanations.notes_quote: where each $S_i$ is a set of propositional valuations.

explanations.text: The ordering represents typicality from most usual to most exceptional.
explanations.notes_quote: $S_1$ contains the most usual worlds; $S_k$ contains the most exceptional cases.

explanations.text: The definition does not require all valuations to appear. Impossible worlds may be omitted entirely.
explanations.notes_quote: the definition does not require that every possible valuation appears in some $S_i$ — models deemed impossible need not appear anywhere.

explanations.text: Impossible models are allowed to be absent from all layers.
explanations.notes_quote: models deemed impossible need not appear anywhere.

questions.text: How is the consequence relation $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$ defined for a ranked preferential model $\vec{S}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ holds if the minimal layer $S_j$ with $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$ satisfies $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$
If no layer contains any model of $F$, then $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ holds vacuously
All layers, not just the first, are checked and $G$ must hold in all of them
The check mirrors the classical $\text{mod}(F) \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$ but restricted to the single most-usual layer $S_j$
$F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ fails if even one layer beyond $S_j$ contains an $F$-world that is not a $G$-world

explanations.text: This is the core definition: check the minimal (most usual) layer containing F-worlds.
explanations.notes_quote: find the first layer $S_j$ that contains at least one valuation satisfying $F$. Then check whether every valuation in $S_j$ that satisfies $F$ also satisfies $G$.

explanations.text: When F is deemed impossible (no F-worlds in any layer), the consequence holds vacuously.
explanations.notes_quote: If no layer contains any model of $F$, then $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ holds automatically (the 'vacuous' case — $F$ is deemed impossible).

explanations.text: Only the single minimal layer $S_j$ is checked — layers below $j$ are empty of F-worlds and later layers are irrelevant.
explanations.notes_quote: find the first layer $S_j$ that contains at least one valuation satisfying $F$.

explanations.text: The definition restricts the classical inclusion check to the most typical layer.
explanations.notes_quote: This mirrors the classical check $\text{mod}(F) \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$, but restricted to the single most-usual layer $S_j$.

explanations.text: Only the minimal layer $S_j$ matters. F-worlds in later layers do not affect the truth of $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$.

questions.text: Which set-theoretic identities for $\text{mod}(\cdot)$ are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) = \text{mod}(F) \cap \text{mod}(G)$
$\text{mod}(F \vee G) = \text{mod}(F) \cup \text{mod}(G)$
$\text{mod}(\neg F) = \overline{\text{mod}(F)}$ (complement of $\text{mod}(F)$)
$\text{mod}(F \wedge G) = \text{mod}(F) \cup \text{mod}(G)$
$\text{mod}(F \to G) = \text{mod}(\neg F) \cup \text{mod}(G)$

explanations.text: Conjunction corresponds to intersection of model sets.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) = \text{mod}(F) \cap \text{mod}(G)$

explanations.text: Disjunction corresponds to union of model sets.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F \vee G) = \text{mod}(F) \cup \text{mod}(G)$

explanations.text: Negation corresponds to complementation.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(\neg F) = \overline{\text{mod}(F)}$

explanations.text: Conjunction corresponds to intersection, not union. Union corresponds to disjunction.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) = \text{mod}(F) \cap \text{mod}(G)$

explanations.text:
$F 	o G \equiv 
eg F \vee G$, so $	ext{mod}(F 	o G) = 	ext{mod}(
eg F) \cup 	ext{mod}(G)$. This follows from the identities for $\vee$ and $
eg$.

questions.text:
Consider $\vec{S} = \langle S_1, S_2, S_3 angle$ with $S_1 = \{(p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r)\}$, $S_2 = \{(
eg p \wedge q \wedge r), (p \wedge q \wedge 
eg r)\}$, $S_3 = \{(p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r)\}$. Which of the following hold? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $p \approxeq_{\vec{S}} \neg q$ is TRUE, because the only $p$-world in $S_1$ satisfies $\neg q$
$(p \wedge r) \approxeq_{\vec{S}} q$ is FALSE, because the $S_3$ world $(p \wedge \neg q \wedge r)$ does not satisfy $q$
$q \approxeq_{\vec{S}} (p \to r)$ is FALSE, because $(p \wedge q \wedge \neg r) \in S_2$ does not satisfy $p \to r$
$(p \wedge r) \approxeq_{\vec{S}} q$ is TRUE because $p \wedge r$ holds in $S_1$
$p \approxeq_{\vec{S}} \neg q$ requires checking $S_2$ and $S_3$ as well as $S_1$

explanations.text: S1 is the minimal layer for p, and (p∧¬q∧¬r) satisfies ¬q.
explanations.notes_quote:
$S_1$ is minimal with $	ext{mod}(p) \cap S_1 
eq \varnothing$. $	ext{mod}(p) \cap S_1 = \{(p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r)\} \subseteq 	ext{mod}(
eg q)$. True.

explanations.text: No p∧r-world exists in S1 or S2; S3 contains (p∧¬q∧r) which does not satisfy q.
explanations.notes_quote: $S_3$ is minimal: $\text{mod}(p \wedge r) \cap S_3 = \{(p \wedge \neg q \wedge r)\} \not\subseteq \text{mod}(q)$. False.

explanations.text: S2 is minimal for q, and (p∧q∧¬r) does not satisfy p→r (since p is true but r is false).
explanations.notes_quote:
$S_2$ is minimal: $	ext{mod}(q) \cap S_2 = \{(
eg p \wedge q \wedge r), (p \wedge q \wedge 
eg r)\}$. Is $(p \wedge q \wedge 
eg r) \in 	ext{mod}(p 	o r)$? No. False.

explanations.text: p∧r has no worlds in S1 (S1 only contains (p∧¬q∧¬r) which has ¬r). The minimal layer for p∧r is S3, not S1.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(p \wedge r) \cap S_1 = \varnothing$

explanations.text: Only the minimal layer S1 is checked for p≈¬q. Once S1 contains a p-world, later layers are irrelevant.
explanations.notes_quote: find the first layer $S_j$ that contains at least one valuation satisfying $F$.

questions.text: What does Theorem 2.3 state? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For any ranked preferential model $\vec{S}$, the relation $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$ is a rational consequence relation
$\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$ satisfies all six Gabbay–Makinson rules for every ranked model $\vec{S}$
Every rational consequence relation is generated by some ranked preferential model
Ranked models are a sound semantics for rational consequence

explanations.text: This is the statement of Theorem 2.3.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $\vec{S} = \langle S_1, \ldots, S_k \rangle$ be any ranked preferential model. Then $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$ is a rational consequence relation.

explanations.text: The proof verifies all GM rules for $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$.
explanations.notes_quote: Proof strategy: Show $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$ satisfies each GM rule.

explanations.text: This is the statement of Theorem 2.4 (Part II), not Theorem 2.3. Theorem 2.3 is the soundness direction only.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $\mid\!\!\sim$ be any rational consequence relation. Then there is some ranked preferential model $\vec{S}$ such that $\approxeq_{\vec{S}} = \mid\!\!\sim$.

explanations.text: Theorem 2.3 establishes soundness: every ranked model generates a rational relation.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $\vec{S} = \langle S_1, \ldots, S_k \rangle$ be any ranked preferential model. Then $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$ is a rational consequence relation.

questions.source_ref: Section 2.2: Theorem 2.3 — Proof of REF and R∧
questions.text: Which correctly describe how Reflexivity (REF) and Right-And (R$\wedge$) are verified for $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: REF: $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F)$ holds trivially for any set

R$\wedge$: If $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$ and $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$, then $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(G) \cap \text{mod}(H) = \text{mod}(G \wedge H)$
REF requires checking all layers, not just the minimal one
R$\wedge$ uses the identity $\text{mod}(G \wedge H) = \text{mod}(G) \cap \text{mod}(H)$

explanations.text: Any set is a subset of itself — reflexivity holds trivially.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$. Then $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F)$ trivially.

explanations.text: This is the set-theoretic argument for R∧.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$ and $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$, so $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(G) \cap \text{mod}(H) = \text{mod}(G \wedge H)$.

explanations.text: Only the minimal layer $S_j$ is used in the proof of REF, not all layers.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$.

explanations.text: The set identity $\text{mod}(G \wedge H) = \text{mod}(G) \cap \text{mod}(H)$ is used to complete the R∧ proof.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(G) \cap \text{mod}(H) = \text{mod}(G \wedge H)$.

questions.text: How is Rational Monotonicity (RMON) proved for $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text:
From $F 
ot\approxeq_{\vec{S}} 
eg G$, there exists some $	ext{mod}(F) \cap S_j 
ot\subseteq 	ext{mod}(
eg G)$, meaning $	ext{mod}(F) \cap 	ext{mod}(G) \cap S_j 
eq \varnothing$

Since $F 
ot\approxeq_{\vec{S}} 
eg G$, all layers before the minimal $F \wedge G$-layer contain only $
eg G$-worlds of $F$, so the minimal $F$-layer equals the minimal $F \wedge G$-layer
At the shared minimal layer: $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$
RMON is proved by showing that the minimal $F \wedge G$-layer is always strictly later than the minimal $F$-layer

explanations.text: The negation of $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} \neg G$ gives a layer where F-worlds include some G-worlds.
explanations.notes_quote:
Since $F 
ot\approxeq_{\vec{S}} 
eg G$, there exists some $	ext{mod}(F) \cap S_j 
ot\subseteq 	ext{mod}(
eg G)$, which means $	ext{mod}(F) \cap 	ext{mod}(G) \cap S_j 
eq \varnothing$.

explanations.text: The RMON proof uses the constraint from $F \not\approxeq_{\vec{S}} \neg G$ to show the minimal layers coincide.
explanations.notes_quote: for all $i < j$: $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_i \subseteq \overline{\text{mod}(G)} = \text{mod}(\neg G)$, which means $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_i = \varnothing$. So $j$ is also the minimal layer for $F$.

explanations.text: Once the layers are equal, the subset chain gives the result.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$.

explanations.text: The proof shows the minimal F-layer and minimal F∧G-layer are the same (equal), not that F∧G's layer is strictly later.
explanations.notes_quote: So $j$ is also the minimal layer for $F$

questions.source_ref: Section 2.2: Application — Showing Consequences Cannot Be Derived
questions.text: How can ranked preferential models be used to show that a conclusion cannot be derived from premises? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Construct a ranked model that satisfies all premises but falsifies the conclusion — this is a counter-model
By Theorem 2.3, every ranked model generates a rational relation; if the conclusion were provable via GM rules, it would hold in every rational relation, hence in this model — contradiction
Find a ranked model where the conclusion holds but the premises fail
Enumerate all possible GM proofs and check none of them prove the conclusion

explanations.text: This is the counter-model method — a ranked model satisfying premises but falsifying the conclusion.
explanations.notes_quote: to show a conclusion $F \approxeq G$ cannot be derived from the GM rules, it suffices to construct a ranked preferential model that satisfies all premises but fails the conclusion.

explanations.text: The contrapositive of Theorem 2.3 + completeness (Theorem 2.4) gives the counter-model method its logical force.
explanations.notes_quote: Since every $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$ is rational, if the conclusion were provable from the premises via GM rules, it would hold in every rational consequence relation — in particular in our constructed model.

explanations.text: The counter-model must satisfy the premises and falsify the conclusion — not the other way around.
Enumerating all GM proofs is not the stated method. The semantic counter-model approach is used instead.

questions.source_ref: Section 2.2: Historical Note — Non-Ranked Preferential Models
questions.text: Which statements correctly compare ranked preferential models with (non-ranked) preferential models? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Ranked models use a total linear ordering on layers; preferential models use a partial order
Preferential models satisfy all GM rules except Rational Monotonicity (RMON)
Ranked models can be expressed as a pair $( \{S_1,\ldots,S_k\}, \prec)$ where $\prec$ is a total linear order
Preferential models satisfy RMON but fail Cautious Monotonicity (CMON)
If RMON is judged unreasonable, preferential models are a viable alternative semantics

explanations.text: The key structural difference: total vs partial order.
explanations.notes_quote: Relaxing to a partial order (reflexive, transitive, anti-symmetric) gives a preferential model

explanations.text: Preferential models satisfy all GM rules except RMON.
explanations.notes_quote: This satisfies all GM rules except Rational Monotonicity (RMON).

explanations.text: Ranked models can be re-expressed using a total linear ordering $\prec$.
explanations.notes_quote: Ranked preferential models $\vec{S} = \langle S_1, \ldots, S_k \rangle$ can be re-expressed as a pair $( \{S_1,\ldots,S_k\}, \prec)$ where $\prec$ is a total linear ordering

explanations.text: Preferential models fail RMON, not CMON. They satisfy CMON.
explanations.notes_quote: This satisfies all GM rules except Rational Monotonicity (RMON).

explanations.text: The notes explicitly present preferential models as an alternative when RMON is disputed.
explanations.notes_quote: If RMON is judged unreasonable, preferential models are a viable alternative.

questions.text: What does Theorem 2.4 (The Representation Theorem) state? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Every rational consequence relation $\mid\!\!\sim$ is generated by some ranked preferential model $\vec{S}$ such that $\approxeq_{\vec{S}} = \mid\!\!\sim$
Theorem 2.4 is the converse of Theorem 2.3
Together, Theorems 2.3 and 2.4 give: a relation is rational if and only if it is generated by some ranked preferential model
Every ranked preferential model generates a rational consequence relation
Theorem 2.4 is a completeness result for ranked preferential models

explanations.text: This is the statement of Theorem 2.4.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $\mid\!\!\sim$ be any rational consequence relation. Then there is some ranked preferential model $\vec{S}$ such that $\approxeq_{\vec{S}} = \mid\!\!\sim$.

explanations.text: Theorem 2.3 goes from model to rational relation; Theorem 2.4 goes the other way.
explanations.notes_quote: This is the converse of Theorem 2.3.

explanations.text: Together the two theorems give the biconditional characterisation.
explanations.notes_quote: Together, Theorems 2.3 and 2.4 give a complete characterisation: a relation is rational if and only if it is generated by some ranked preferential model.

explanations.text: That is Theorem 2.3, not Theorem 2.4. Theorem 2.4 goes from rational relation to ranked model.
Theorem 2.4 establishes completeness: every rational relation has a ranked model representation.
explanations.notes_quote: This is the converse of Theorem 2.3. Together, Theorems 2.3 and 2.4 give a complete characterisation

questions.text: In the constructive proof sketch of Theorem 2.4, how is $S_1$ (the first layer) determined? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $S_1 = \bigcap \{\text{mod}(F) : \top \mid\!\!\sim F\}$ — the intersection of models of all formulas that usually follow from a tautology
$S_1$ contains the most usual worlds: all valuations satisfying every formula $F$ such that $\top \mid\!\!\sim F$
$S_1$ is constructed by taking the union of all formula models whose negation is usually true
The construction terminates in finitely many steps because there are only finitely many propositional valuations (pigeonhole principle)

explanations.text: This is the explicit formula for S1 in the proof sketch.
explanations.notes_quote: $S_1 = \bigcap\{\text{mod}(F) : \top \mid\!\!\sim F\}$

explanations.text: S1 captures the most typical worlds by intersecting all usualness constraints from the tautology.
explanations.notes_quote: The most usual worlds are those in which every 'usually true' formula (i.e. every $F$ such that $\top \mid\!\!\sim F$) holds

explanations.text: S1 is defined via intersection of models of formulas that usually follow from $\top$, not via negation or union.
explanations.notes_quote: $S_1 = \bigcap\{\text{mod}(F) : \top \mid\!\!\sim F\}$

explanations.text: Finiteness of propositional valuations guarantees the pigeonhole-based termination.
explanations.notes_quote: Since there are only finitely many propositional valuations, by the pigeonhole principle, there must be some finite $k$ after which the construction generates no further non-empty sets

questions.text: Which structural properties of the layers $S_i$ are used in the proof of Theorem 2.4? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(\Theta_j)$ follows from the construction
The layers are disjoint: $S_i \cap S_j = \varnothing$ for $i < j$ (i.e., $i \neq j$)
The family $\{\text{mod}(F) : \Theta_i \mid\!\!\sim F\}$ is closed under intersections (by R$\wedge$)
Each layer $S_i$ is a superset of $S_{i+1}$

explanations.text: The construction ensures each layer is inside the corresponding $\Theta_j$-region.
explanations.notes_quote: $S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(\Theta_j)$ (follows from the construction)

explanations.text: The layers partition the valuations they contain — no valuation is in two layers.
explanations.notes_quote: $S_i \cap S_j = \varnothing$ for $i < j$ (the layers are disjoint)

explanations.text: Closure under intersections (from R∧) ensures the intersection $S_i$ is itself representable.
explanations.notes_quote: The set $\{\text{mod}(F) : \Theta_i \mid\!\!\sim F\}$ is finite and closed under intersections (by (R$\wedge$))

explanations.text: There is no nesting requirement between layers. The layers are disjoint sets, not nested.

questions.text: Which statements correctly describe the derived rule Negation Rationality (NRAT)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text:
NRAT states: if $(F \wedge G) 
ot\mid\!\!\sim H$ and $(F \wedge 
eg G) 
ot\mid\!\!\sim H$, then $F 
ot\mid\!\!\sim H$
NRAT is a derived rule that follows from the Representation Theorem (Theorem 2.4)
NRAT allows deriving that $F \mid\!\!\sim H$ whenever its two conjunctive splits both fail the consequence
NRAT is one of the six original Gabbay–Makinson axioms

explanations.text: This is the statement of NRAT as given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote:
$(F \wedge G) 
ot\mid\!\!\sim H \quad (F \wedge 
eg G) 
ot\mid\!\!\sim H$ over $F 
ot\mid\!\!\sim H$ (NRAT)

explanations.text: NRAT is derived using the Representation Theorem, not from the GM axioms directly.
explanations.notes_quote: By the Representation Theorem, the following rule holds for all rational consequence relations

explanations.text: NRAT concludes $F \not\mid\!\!\sim H$ (negated consequence), not $F \mid\!\!\sim H$. The rule is about when a consequence fails.
explanations.notes_quote:
$(F \wedge G) 
ot\mid\!\!\sim H \quad (F \wedge 
eg G) 
ot\mid\!\!\sim H$ over $F 
ot\mid\!\!\sim H$

explanations.text: NRAT is a derived rule, not one of the six original GM axioms (REF, R∧, L∨, LLE, RWE, CMON, RMON).

questions.text: Which statements correctly describe the vacuous case for $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_i = \varnothing$ for all $i$, then $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ holds for any $G$
The vacuous case arises when $F$ is deemed impossible — no world in any layer satisfies $F$
In the vacuous case, the truth of $G$ must still be checked in all layers
The vacuous case mirrors classical logic: from $\bot \models G$ for any $G$

explanations.text: The vacuous case makes any consequence vacuously true.
explanations.notes_quote: If no layer contains any model of $F$, then $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ holds automatically (the 'vacuous' case — $F$ is deemed impossible).

explanations.text: Vacuous truth corresponds to F being impossible in the model.
explanations.notes_quote: the 'vacuous' case — $F$ is deemed impossible

explanations.text: In the vacuous case, no checking of G is needed at all — the consequence holds automatically.
explanations.notes_quote: $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ holds automatically

explanations.text: The vacuous case is the non-monotonic analogue of ex falso (anything follows from the impossible).

questions.text: In Question 4.2 (the module premises problem), which statements about the counter-model $\vec{S} = \langle S_1, S_2 \rangle$ are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: P1 ($c \mid\!\!\sim ls$) is verified because the only $c$-world in $S_1$ satisfies $ls$
P3 ($l \mid\!\!\sim mr$) is verified because both $l$-worlds in $S_2$ satisfy $mr$
Conclusion 1 ($mr \mid\!\!\sim \neg c$) fails because $S_2$ contains a $mr$-world with $c$ true
Conclusion 2 ($mr \mid\!\!\sim c$) fails because $S_2$ contains a $mr$-world with $\neg c$
The existence of this counter-model proves that neither conclusion can ever be derived from these premises

explanations.text: S1's only world (c∧ls∧¬mr∧¬l) satisfies both c and ls.
explanations.notes_quote:
P1: $c \mid\!\!\sim ls$ — $S_1$ is minimal with $	ext{mod}(c) \cap S_1 
eq \varnothing$. $	ext{mod}(c) \cap S_1 = \{(c \wedge ls \wedge 
eg mr \wedge 
eg l)\} \subseteq 	ext{mod}(ls)$.

explanations.text: Both l-worlds in S2 have mr=true.
explanations.notes_quote:
P3: $l \mid\!\!\sim mr$ — $S_2$ is minimal. $	ext{mod}(l) \cap S_2 = \{(c \wedge 
eg ls \wedge mr \wedge l), (
eg c \wedge 
eg ls \wedge mr \wedge l)\} \subseteq 	ext{mod}(mr)$.

explanations.text: (c∧¬ls∧mr∧l) ∈ S2 satisfies mr but not ¬c, so the conclusion fails.
explanations.notes_quote:
Conclusion 1 fails: $mr 
ot\mid\!\!\sim 
eg c$ — $S_2$ is minimal for $mr$. $	ext{mod}(mr) \cap S_2 
ot\subseteq 	ext{mod}(
eg c)$.

explanations.text: (¬c∧¬ls∧mr∧l) ∈ S2 satisfies mr but not c, so conclusion 2 fails.
explanations.notes_quote:
Conclusion 2 fails: $mr 
ot\mid\!\!\sim c$ — same layer; $	ext{mod}(mr) \cap S_2 
ot\subseteq 	ext{mod}(c)$ since $(
eg c \wedge 
eg ls \wedge mr \wedge l) \in 	ext{mod}(
eg c)$.

explanations.text: By Theorems 2.3 and 2.4, if a conclusion were derivable from premises via GM rules, it would hold in all rational relations including this one — but it doesn't.
explanations.notes_quote: By the Representation Theorem, every consequence relation generated by a ranked preferential model is rational. Hence if either conclusion were derivable from the premises via the GM rules, it would hold in this model — but it doesn't.

questions.text: Question 4.3 shows that classical consequence $\models$ is rational. Which statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Take $\vec{S} = (S_1)$ where $S_1$ is the set of all possible propositional valuations
For this model: $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_1 = \text{mod}(F)$ for any formula $F$
$F \models G$ iff $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ in this single-layer model with all valuations
Classical consequence is a special case of rational consequence — the most credulous possible
This model has multiple layers, one for each atomic proposition

explanations.text: The construction uses a single layer containing all valuations.
explanations.notes_quote: Take $\vec{S} = (S_1)$ where $S_1$ is the set of all possible propositional valuations.

explanations.text: Since S1 contains all valuations, intersecting with S1 gives back the full model set.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_1 = \text{mod}(F)$ for any $F$.

explanations.text: The proof shows the two coincide for this model.
explanations.notes_quote: Proof ($F \models G \Leftrightarrow F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$)

explanations.text: The notes do not characterise classical consequence as 'most credulous' — this is not stated. Classical consequence is shown to be a special case of rational consequence, but the specific framing is not given.
The model has exactly one layer S1 (not multiple layers, one per proposition).
explanations.notes_quote: Take $\vec{S} = (S_1)$

questions.text: Question 4.4(i) proves Supra-classicality (SC) via the Representation Theorem. Which steps are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Assume $F \models G$, i.e. $\text{mod}(F) \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$
For any ranked model $\vec{S}$: either no layer contains an $F$-world (done), or the minimal layer satisfies $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F) \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$
Since $\vec{S}$ is arbitrary and every rational relation arises from some $\vec{S}$, we conclude $F \mid\!\!\sim G$
SC is proved by induction on the number of layers in $\vec{S}$

explanations.text: The proof begins with the classical consequence assumption.
explanations.notes_quote: Suppose $F \models G$, i.e. $\text{mod}(F) \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$.

explanations.text: The two cases (vacuous and non-vacuous) are handled, using the classical inclusion.
explanations.notes_quote: If $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_i = \varnothing$ for all $i$, done. Otherwise let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$: $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F) \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$

explanations.text: The Representation Theorem allows lifting from all ranked models to all rational relations.
explanations.notes_quote: Since $\vec{S}$ is arbitrary and every rational consequence relation arises from some $\vec{S}$, we have $F \mid\!\!\sim G$.

explanations.text: The proof is not by induction on layers — it directly handles two cases (vacuous and non-vacuous) using set inclusion.

questions.text: In the proof of Conditionalisation (CON) for ranked models, which steps are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Assume $(F \wedge G) \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$; let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$
If $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$: the hypothesis gives $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H) \subseteq \text{mod}(G \to H)$
If $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j = \varnothing$: then $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(\neg G) = \text{mod}(G \to H)$
In both subcases, $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(G \to H)$, giving $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} (G \to H)$
Conditionalisation requires that $G$ is classically valid for the proof to work

explanations.text: This is the setup for the CON proof.
explanations.notes_quote: Suppose $(F \wedge G) \mid\!\!\sim H$, i.e. $(F \wedge G) \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$ for an arbitrary ranked model $\vec{S}$. Let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$.

explanations.text: First subcase: F∧G-worlds exist in the minimal F-layer.
explanations.notes_quote: If $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$: by hypothesis, $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H) \subseteq \text{mod}(G \to H)$.

explanations.text: Second subcase: no F∧G-worlds in the minimal F-layer, so all F-worlds in that layer satisfy ¬G, hence G→H.
explanations.notes_quote: If $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j = \varnothing$: then $\text{mod}(F) \cap \text{mod}(G) \cap S_j = \varnothing$, so $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \overline{\text{mod}(G)} = \text{mod}(\neg G) \subseteq \text{mod}(G \to H)$.

explanations.text: Both subcases conclude mod(F)∩S_j ⊆ mod(G→H), so F≈(G→H) holds.
explanations.notes_quote: In both cases, $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(G \to H)$, giving $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} (G \to H)$.

explanations.text: There is no requirement for G to be classically valid. The proof works for any G.

questions.text: In the proof of Cautious Cut (CC) for ranked models, which steps are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Assume $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ and $(F \wedge G) \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$; let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$
Since $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_i \subseteq \text{mod}(F) \cap S_i = \varnothing$ for all $i < j$, $j$ is also minimal for $F \wedge G$
From $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$: $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$, so $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$
Hence $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$, giving $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$
Cautious Cut cannot be proved via the Representation Theorem and requires a direct GM proof

explanations.text: This is the setup for the CC proof.
explanations.notes_quote: Suppose $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ and $(F \wedge G) \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$. Let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$.

explanations.text: F∧G-worlds are a subset of F-worlds, so if F has no worlds before layer j, neither does F∧G.
explanations.notes_quote: Since $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_i \subseteq \text{mod}(F) \cap S_i = \varnothing$ for all $i < j$, it follows that $j$ is also minimal with $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$.

explanations.text: The inclusion chain connects F-worlds at layer j through G to F∧G and then to H.
explanations.notes_quote: From $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$: $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(G)$. Therefore: $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F) \cap \text{mod}(G) \cap S_j = \text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$

explanations.text: The final step concludes F≈H from the inclusion chain.
explanations.notes_quote: Hence $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$.

explanations.text: The proof of CC given in Question 4.4(iii) is precisely via the Representation Theorem approach (working with an arbitrary ranked model).

questions.text: Which statements correctly define or describe Supra-classicality (SC), Conditionalisation (CON), and Cautious Cut (CC)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: SC: If $F \models G$ classically, then $F \mid\!\!\sim G$
CON: If $(F \wedge G) \mid\!\!\sim H$, then $F \mid\!\!\sim (G \to H)$
CC: If $F \mid\!\!\sim G$ and $(F \wedge G) \mid\!\!\sim H$, then $F \mid\!\!\sim H$
SC states that rational consequence is strictly weaker than classical consequence
CON is also called Conditionalisation and is a derived rule provable via the Representation Theorem

explanations.text: SC states rational consequence extends classical consequence.
explanations.notes_quote: Supra-classicality (SC): If $F \models G$ classically, then $F \mid\!\!\sim G$; rational consequence extends classical consequence

explanations.text: This is the statement of CON.
explanations.notes_quote: Conditionalisation (CON): If $(F \wedge G) \mid\!\!\sim H$ then $F \mid\!\!\sim (G \to H)$

explanations.text: This is the statement of CC.
explanations.notes_quote: Cautious Cut (CC): If $F \mid\!\!\sim G$ and $(F \wedge G) \mid\!\!\sim H$ then $F \mid\!\!\sim H$

explanations.text: SC says rational consequence extends classical consequence — rational is at least as strong, not strictly weaker. Every classical consequence is also a rational consequence.
explanations.notes_quote: rational consequence extends classical consequence

explanations.text: CON is proved as a derived rule in Question 4.4(ii) using the Representation Theorem.

questions.text: Which statements correctly define key terms from Week 4? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\text{mod}(F)$ is the set of all propositional valuations satisfying formula $F$
A rational consequence relation satisfies all six Gabbay–Makinson rules: REF, R$\wedge$, L$\vee$, LLE, RWE, CMON, RMON
The counter-model method: to show $F \mid\!\!\sim G$ is not derivable from premises, construct a ranked model satisfying the premises but falsifying $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$
A preferential model uses a total linear ordering over layers, like a ranked model
The Representation Theorem: a relation is rational iff it is generated by some ranked preferential model

explanations.text: This is the standard definition of mod(F).
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F)$: The set of all propositional valuations satisfying formula $F$

explanations.text: The GM rules are the defining axioms of rational consequence.
explanations.notes_quote: Rational consequence relation: A consequence relation satisfying all six Gabbay–Makinson rules: REF, R$\wedge$, L$\vee$, LLE, RWE, CMON, RMON

explanations.text: This is the definition of the counter-model method.
explanations.notes_quote: Counter-model method: To show a conclusion $F \mid\!\!\sim G$ cannot be derived from premises using GM rules: construct a ranked model satisfying the premises but falsifying $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$

explanations.text: A preferential model uses a partial order, not a total linear order. The total linear order distinguishes ranked models.
explanations.notes_quote: Preferential model: Like a ranked model but with a partial order instead of total linear order

explanations.text: This is the combined statement of Theorems 2.3 and 2.4.
explanations.notes_quote: Representation Theorem: Every rational consequence relation $\mid\!\!\sim$ is generated by some ranked preferential model $\vec{S}$, i.e. $\mid\!\!\sim = \approxeq_{\vec{S}}$. Combined with Theorem 2.3: rational $\Leftrightarrow$ rankedly representable

questions.text: Which of the following correctly characterise the relationship between ranked preferential models and rational consequence relations? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Every ranked preferential model generates a rational consequence relation (Theorem 2.3 — soundness)
Every rational consequence relation is generated by some ranked preferential model (Theorem 2.4 — completeness)
Rational consequence and ranked preferential models are in a one-to-one correspondence: each rational relation comes from exactly one ranked model
To prove a rule holds for all rational relations, it suffices to prove it holds for all ranked preferential models
To show a rule does not hold for all rational relations, it suffices to find one ranked model where it fails

explanations.text: Theorem 2.3 is the soundness direction.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $\vec{S} = \langle S_1, \ldots, S_k \rangle$ be any ranked preferential model. Then $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$ is a rational consequence relation.

explanations.text: Theorem 2.4 is the completeness direction.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $\mid\!\!\sim$ be any rational consequence relation. Then there is some ranked preferential model $\vec{S}$ such that $\approxeq_{\vec{S}} = \mid\!\!\sim$.

explanations.text: The correspondence is not necessarily one-to-one. Different ranked models may generate the same rational relation. Theorem 2.4 guarantees existence, not uniqueness.
By Theorem 2.4, all rational relations come from ranked models, so proving something for all ranked models proves it for all rational relations.
By Theorem 2.3, ranked models give rational relations; a counterexample in a ranked model is a counterexample in a rational relation.
explanations.notes_quote: to show a conclusion $F \approxeq G$ cannot be derived from the GM rules, it suffices to construct a ranked preferential model that satisfies all premises but fails the conclusion.

questions.text: In the proof of Cautious Monotonicity (CMON) for $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$, which steps are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Assume $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} G$ and $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$; let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$
$\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j = (\text{mod}(F) \cap \text{mod}(G)) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$
Hence $F \wedge G \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$, establishing CMON
CMON requires checking all layers, not just the minimal $F \wedge G$-layer

explanations.text: This is the setup for the CMON proof.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$.

explanations.text: The subset chain is the core of the CMON argument.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j = (\text{mod}(F) \cap \text{mod}(G)) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$.

explanations.text: The conclusion $F \wedge G \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$ follows from the inclusion.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F \wedge G) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$.

explanations.text: Only the minimal F∧G-layer is checked. The definition of $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$ always uses only the minimal relevant layer.

questions.text: In the proof of Left-Or (L$\vee$) for $\approxeq_{\vec{S}}$, which steps are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Assume $F \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$ and $G \approxeq_{\vec{S}} H$; let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F \vee G) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$
$\text{mod}(F \vee G) \cap S_j = (\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j) \cup (\text{mod}(G) \cap S_j)$
If $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$, then $j$ is also the minimal layer for $F$, so $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$
L$\vee$ requires both $F$ and $G$ to have models in the same minimal layer $S_j$

explanations.text: This is the setup for the L∨ proof.
explanations.notes_quote: Let $j$ be minimal with $\text{mod}(F \vee G) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$.

explanations.text: Since mod(F∨G) = mod(F)∪mod(G), intersecting with S_j distributes.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{mod}(F \vee G) \cap S_j = (\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j) \cup (\text{mod}(G) \cap S_j) \neq \varnothing$

explanations.text: If F has a model at layer j, since all earlier layers have no F∨G worlds (and hence no F worlds), j is minimal for F too.
explanations.notes_quote: If $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \neq \varnothing$, then $j$ is also minimal for $F$, so $\text{mod}(F) \cap S_j \subseteq \text{mod}(H)$.

explanations.text: L∨ does not require both F and G to have models in S_j. The proof handles the case where only one of them contributes models at layer j.

questions.text: Which statements correctly summarise Week 4's main results? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Ranked preferential models provide a sound and complete semantics for rational consequence
The Representation Theorem enables the proof method: to prove a rule for all rational relations, prove it for all ranked models; to refute, construct a ranked counter-model
Classical consequence $\models$ is a special case of rational consequence, generated by the single-layer model with all valuations
NRAT is one of the six original GM axioms that defines rational consequence
The layers in a ranked model must be non-empty — every layer $S_i$ must contain at least one valuation

explanations.text: Theorems 2.3 and 2.4 together establish soundness and completeness.
explanations.notes_quote: This week established the semantic foundation of rational consequence

explanations.text: The Representation Theorem is the key proof tool enabling both directions of argumentation.
explanations.notes_quote: LO4 (Apply Representation Theorem): Theorem 2.4 is the converse: every rational relation comes from some ranked model. This gives a powerful proof tool — to prove a rule holds for all rational relations, prove it holds for all ranked models. To show a conclusion is not provable, construct a ranked counter-model.

explanations.text: Question 4.3 established this result explicitly.
explanations.notes_quote: Classical consequence $\models$ was shown to be rational (4.3) via the single-layer model with all valuations.

explanations.text: NRAT is a derived rule following from the Representation Theorem, not one of the six original GM axioms.
explanations.notes_quote: By the Representation Theorem, the following rule holds for all rational consequence relations

explanations.text: The definition does not require all layers to be non-empty. In the proof of Theorem 2.4, the construction terminates when empty layers are reached.
explanations.notes_quote: there must be some finite $k$ after which the construction generates no further non-empty sets: $S_{k+1} = S_{k+2} = \cdots = \varnothing$
